text: Politics is fundamentally a communicative act. However, due to the massive quantity of political speech and in particular political writing, analysis of this speech has generally been thought to be beyond the capacity of any reasonable research effort. Because political science as a discipline has long acknowledged the importance of political text, there have been few attempts to develop politically relevant data for analysis because to do so required the expenditure of huge resources of time and money. High scale computational costs have been so high that relatively few attempts have been made to collect the large bodies of data that would be required for serious scientific development of the method. Finally, the advent of cheaper and easier high scale computational text analysis tools and services means that political science based on text analysis is feasible at any number of scales and many techniques have now been proven to have at least some value. Nevertheless, use of any new tool as a serious substitute for human effort, analysis, or insight is contingent on that tool being more than trivial to use, less expensive than hand-driven alternatives, and on thorough knowledge of limitations and problems with such use of new technologies. Here, our goal is to introduce political science and a new generation of new quantitative text analyses. We also explain practices for validating findings that they yield; we also discuss a little of what is misleading in the literature associated with these methods and illustrate possible procedures with data from a project the first author has been leading on partisan media. Finally, for automation of these methods to become the routine tools they might be, the techniques and associated validation procedures have to be refined and a common political science vocabulary regarding use of the techniques in place of more traditional, “better,” human methods of qualitative data analysis will have to be established and agreed upon.
